meta.title: Mercedes-Benz M102 Engine — Specs, Reliability & Replacement Cost

hero.h1: Mercedes-Benz M102 Engine — Specs, Reliability & Replacement Cost
hero.subHeadline: The Mercedes M102 is the durable four‑cylinder that powered 1980s Mercedes saloons — reliable but carburettor and timing chain tensioner issues are well known. Backed by real UK enquiries [ESTIMATED].

verdictRating.confidence: 82% confidence — based on owner reports and specialist workshop feedback across multiple generations.

rows.score: 20 (Most failures are simple mechanical repairs; carb and injection parts readily available)
16 (Parts available but some specific fuel injection components are getting scarce)
16 (Recon cost ~£2,200 against lowest host value ~£4,000 = 55% (45–59% band → 16))

verdictRating.oneLineVerdict: "The M102 is a robust, old‑school four‑cylinder — carburettor and timing chain tensioner wear are its main weaknesses — buy with documented carb/injection service history."
verdictRating.bestFor: Classic Mercedes enthusiasts wanting a simple, rebuildable engine with strong parts support.
verdictRating.avoidIf: Seeking a low‑maintenance daily driver with modern fuel efficiency and electronics.

costGuide.labourEstimate: Fitting an M102 typically takes 8–12 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [ESTIMATED].

cta.label: → Compare M102 engine prices from UK specialists

items.question: Cost — "How much does an M102 engine replacement cost?"
items.answer: A reconditioned M102 typically costs £1,800–£2,800 supply only [ESTIMATED], varying by displacement (2.0L vs 2.3L) and injection type. Fitting adds £600–£1,200 depending on labour rates. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

items.question: Reliability — "Is the M102 a reliable engine?"
items.answer: The M102 is widely regarded as one of Mercedes' most reliable four‑cylinders — many examples have covered 300,000+ miles with regular maintenance. Carburettor and fuel injection systems require periodic attention, but the bottom end is extremely durable.

items.question: Common failure — "What is the most common M102 failure?"
items.answer: Timing chain tensioner wear is the most critical issue — a rattling noise on start‑up indicates chain stretch, which can lead to jumped timing. Carburettor and early injection system (KE‑Jetronic) also suffer from age‑related seal and diaphragm failures.

items.question: Fitment — "Which Mercedes-Benz models use the M102?"
items.answer: The M102 powered the W123 and W124 E‑Class, the W201 190 series, and late‑production W114 saloons — essentially the entire mainstream Mercedes saloon range from 1980 to 1993.

items.question: Longevity — "How long does the M102 last?"
items.answer: With proper maintenance, M102 engines routinely exceed 300,000 miles — the bottom end is over‑engineered and many examples are still on the road today. Regular oil changes and timing chain checks are the key to long life.
